module_description: The very basics — what Python is, how to write your first line of code, and how computers think.

concepts.title: What is Python?
concepts.mastery_definition: Kid can explain what Python is and name one thing it can do

analogies.text: Python is like a recipe. You write instructions in a language the computer understands, and it follows them exactly — step by step.
Think of Python like a TV remote. The remote has buttons that tell the TV what to do. Python has commands that tell the computer what to do.
Python is a language — just like English or Spanish — except instead of talking to people, you use it to talk to computers.

questions.question: What does Python do when you write instructions for it?
options: It follows them step by step
It sends them to a friend
questions.answer: It follows them step by step
questions.explanation: Python is a programming language — it reads your instructions and follows them exactly, one at a time.

questions.question: Which of these best describes what Python is?
options: A language to give instructions to a computer
questions.answer: A language to give instructions to a computer
questions.explanation: Python is a programming language. Just like you use English to talk to people, you use Python to talk to computers.

questions.question: Who uses Python in real life?
options: Game makers, scientists, app builders, and more
questions.answer: Game makers, scientists, app builders, and more
questions.explanation: Python is used all over the world — to build websites, games, AI, and even control robots!

concepts.title: The print() function
concepts.mastery_definition: Kid can use print() to display text and numbers correctly

analogies.text: print() is like a megaphone. Whatever you put inside the megaphone, everyone can hear it — whatever you put inside print(), the computer shows it on screen.
Think of print() like texting. You type something, hit send, and it appears on the other person's screen. Python's print() sends your message to the screen.
print() is the computer's way of talking back to you. You tell it something, and it shows it on screen so you can see it.

questions.question: What does this code print?
questions.explanation: print() shows exactly what's inside the quotes. Capital letters matter — H and W are uppercase here.

questions.question: Fill in the blank to make this code display the message.
questions.explanation: print() is the command that displays text on screen. Always followed by parentheses.

questions.question: This code has a bug. What is wrong?
options: The word Hello needs to be longer
print should be lowercase, not Print
questions.answer: print should be lowercase, not Print
questions.explanation: Python is case-sensitive. print() must be all lowercase. Print() with a capital P will cause an error.

questions.question: What does this print?
questions.explanation: print() can show numbers too — without quotes. It displays the number 42.

questions.question: Write code that prints your name on the screen.
questions.explanation: Use print() with your name inside quotes. Like: print("Your Name")

concepts.title: Variables — storing information
concepts.mastery_definition: Kid can create a variable, assign a value, and use it in print()

analogies.text: A variable is like a box with a label on it. You put something inside — a number or a word — and any time you need it, you open the box by saying the label.
Think of a variable like a contact in your phone. 'Mom' stores a number. You don't memorize the digits — you just say 'Mom' and your phone knows.
A variable is like a name tag. You stick it on something — a number, a word — so you can find it later by its name.

questions.question: What does this code print?
questions.explanation: We stored "Aria" in a variable called name. When we print(name), Python looks inside the box labeled name and shows what's there.

questions.question: Fill in the blank so the variable age stores the number 10.
questions.explanation: To store a number, just write it directly — no quotes needed. age = 10 stores the number 10.

questions.question: This code has a bug. Find it.
questions.explanation: Variable names are case-sensitive. We created name (lowercase) but tried to use Name (uppercase). They're different to Python.

questions.question: What does this print?
questions.explanation: When you assign a new value to a variable, the old one is replaced. score was 100, then changed to 200. Python shows the latest value.

questions.question: Create a variable called city that stores the name of your favorite city. Then print it.
questions.explanation: First create the variable: city = "Chicago". Then display it: print(city).

concepts.title: Data types — text (strings)
concepts.mastery_definition: Kid knows strings are text, must use quotes, and can create and print a string

analogies.text: A string is like a bead necklace — each bead is one letter, and they're all strung together to make a word or sentence.
Strings are just text in quotes. The quotes are like a pair of bookends — they show Python where your text starts and ends.
Think of a string like a sign you hold up. The quotes are the frame of the sign, and whatever's inside is your message.

questions.question: Which of these is a string in Python?
questions.explanation: A string is text wrapped in quotes. "Hello" has quotes, so it's a string. 42 and 3.14 are numbers. True is a boolean.

questions.question: What does this print?
questions.explanation: The variable greeting stores the string. print() shows the contents without the quotes.

questions.question: What is wrong with this code?
options: message is a bad variable name
questions.explanation: Text in Python must always be in quotes. Without quotes, Python thinks Hello is a variable name, not text.

questions.question: Fill in the blank with your favorite food to complete the string.
questions.explanation: Any word goes inside the quotes to make a string. pizza, tacos, sushi — anything works!

concepts.title: Data types — numbers (integers)
concepts.mastery_definition: Kid knows integers are whole numbers, no quotes needed, and can do basic math

analogies.text: Integers are whole numbers — like the score in a game. No decimal points, no fractions. Just solid, complete numbers.
Think of integers like counting on your fingers. 1, 2, 3 — whole numbers you can count. No halves.
Integers in Python are just numbers without quotes. If you put quotes, Python thinks it's text, not a number you can do math with.

questions.question: What does this print?
questions.explanation: x is 5 and y is 3. Python adds them: 5 + 3 = 8. Numbers without quotes can be used in math.

questions.question: What does this print?
questions.explanation: When numbers are in quotes, they're strings — not numbers. Adding strings joins them together: "5" + "3" = "53", not 8.

questions.question: This code causes an error. Why?
options: You can't add a string and a number
questions.answer: You can't add a string and a number
questions.explanation: score is a string (has quotes) and bonus is an integer. Python can't add them. Fix: score = 100 — remove the quotes.

questions.question: Write code that stores your age in a variable and prints it.
questions.explanation: Store your age without quotes — it's a number. age = 11 then print(age).

concepts.title: Getting input from the user
concepts.mastery_definition: Kid can use input() to collect a response and store it in a variable

analogies.text: input() is like a question box that pops up and waits for you to type something. Whatever you type goes into a variable.
Think of input() like a form you fill out. The program asks a question, you type your answer, and the program remembers what you said.
input() pauses the program and listens. It's the program saying 'your turn' — and it waits until you type something and press Enter.

questions.code:
name = input("What is your name? ")
print("Hello, " + name)
questions.question: If the user types 'Aria', what gets printed?
questions.explanation: input() stores whatever the user typed in the variable name. Then we join "Hello, " with the name to print a greeting.

questions.code:
color = _____("What is your favorite color? ")
print(color)
questions.question: Fill in the blank to ask the user for their favorite color.
questions.explanation: input() is the function that asks the user a question and waits for them to type an answer.

questions.question: What does input() always return?
questions.explanation: input() always gives you text — even if the user types a number like 5, it comes back as the string "5", not the number 5.

questions.question: Write code that asks the user for their favorite animal and then prints: 'Cool! I like [animal] too!'
questions.sample_answer:
animal = input("What is your favorite animal? ")
print("Cool! I like " + animal + " too!")
questions.explanation: Get the input, store it, then use it in your print statement joined with the other text.

concepts.title: Comments — notes in your code
concepts.mastery_definition: Kid can write a comment using # and knows Python ignores comments

analogies.text: Comments are like sticky notes on your code. You write them to remind yourself (or others) what the code does — the computer ignores them completely.
Think of a comment like whispering to yourself while following a recipe. 'This step is for thickening the sauce' — you're reminding yourself, not giving the recipe a new instruction.
A # starts a comment — like putting text in invisible ink. You can read it, but the computer skips right over it.

questions.question: What does this code print?
questions.explanation: The # line is a comment — Python ignores it. Only print("Hello!") runs.

questions.question: What is the purpose of comments in code?
options: To explain what the code does for humans reading it
questions.answer: To explain what the code does for humans reading it
questions.explanation: Comments help you and others understand your code. Python ignores them — they're notes for people, not the computer.

questions.code:
___ Ask the user for their name
name = input("Name: ")
questions.question: Fill in the blank to make this line a comment.
questions.explanation: The # symbol starts a comment. Everything after # on that line is ignored by Python.

concepts.title: Mini Project 1 — Personal Greeting Card
concepts.mastery_definition: Kid builds a working greeting card program using all M1 concepts

mini_project.title: Personal Greeting Card
mini_project.description: Build a program that asks the user their name and age, then prints a personalized greeting card.
mini_project.sample_solution:
# Personal Greeting Card
name = input("What is your name? ")
age = input("How old are you? ")
print("================================")
print("Hello, " + name + "!")
print("Happy " + age + "th birthday!")
print("Welcome to Python programming!")
print("================================")
mini_project.success_criteria: Program asks for name, asks for age, prints a greeting using both values
